s2.q: Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) is BEST described as:
options: A true mechanical obstruction of pulmonary arteries by fetal debris
An anaphylactoid reaction to fetal fluid/tissue entering maternal circulation via the placental bed
s2.explain: AFE is an anaphylactoid (not purely embolic) reaction to fetal antigens. Modern term: 'anaphylactoid syndrome of pregnancy'.

s2.q: What is the approximate incidence of AFE?
s2.explain: AFE is rare (~1-2 per 40,000 deliveries) but accounts for 5-15% of maternal deaths in developed countries.

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s2.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s2.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s2.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s2.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s3.q: Which TWO conditions allow amniotic fluid to enter maternal circulation?
options: Increased intrauterine pressure and opened uterine/endocervical veins
Decreased intrauterine pressure and placental abruption
s3.explain: Two gateway mechanisms: (1) increased intrauterine pressure (abruption, oxytocin overdose), (2) opened uterine/endocervical veins (lacerations, rupture).

s3.q: Meconium in the setting of AFE:
options: Is the primary cause of AFE
Potentiates the toxic nature of amniotic fluid and worsens the condition
Has no effect on the severity
s3.explain: Meconium potentiates, does not initiate. It contains vasoactive substances that amplify the anaphylactoid reaction.

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s3.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s3.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s3.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s3.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s4.q: The pathophysiologic cascade of AFE begins with entry of AF into maternal veins, followed by:
options: Acute cor pulmonale → anaphylaxis → DIC → hypoxia → death
Acute anaphylactoid reaction → pulmonary vascular obstruction → acute cor pulmonale → hypoxia/cardiogenic shock → DIC → coma/death
Hypoxia → DIC → anaphylaxis → cor pulmonale → death
s4.explain: Correct cascade: Entry → Anaphylaxis → Pulmonary obstruction → Cor pulmonale → Hypoxia/Shock → DIC → Coma/death.

s4.q: In AFE, acute cor pulmonale results from:
options: Abrupt increase in right ventricular afterload with decreased LV preload
s4.explain: Pulmonary vascular obstruction causes abrupt ↑ RV afterload and ↓ LV preload, leading to acute cor pulmonale.

s4.q: The final common pathway before death in AFE is:
options: Hypoxic brain injury and multi-organ failure with DIC
s4.explain: Step 7 of the cascade: deep coma → death from hypoxic brain injury and multi-organ failure. Immediate death occurs in >50% of cases.

s4.q: The clinical significance of this section is:

s4.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s4.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s4.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s5.q: Which of the following is a risk factor for AFE?
s5.explain: Placental abruption is a key risk factor as disruption of the placental barrier allows AF entry. Other RFs include operative delivery, cervical lacerations.

s5.q: Which of the following BEST describes AFE risk factor timing?
options: Only occurs in the first trimester
Associated with conditions that disrupt the placental barrier or increase intrauterine pressure
s5.explain: Any condition disrupting the placental bed (abruption, trauma, lacerations) or raising intrauterine pressure can precipitate AFE.

s6.q: The classic clinical triad of AFE is:
s6.explain: The classic triad: hypoxia (severe dyspnea/cyanosis) + cardiovascular collapse + DIC, occurring within minutes of a difficult delivery.

s6.q: The hallmark cardiovascular finding in AFE is:
options: Hypotension, tachycardia, and signs of right heart failure
s6.explain: Cardiovascular: hypotension, tachycardia, signs of right heart failure from acute cor pulmonale.

s6.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s6.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s7.q: Which laboratory finding is most consistent with DIC in AFE?
options: Prolonged PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer
s7.explain: DIC shows: ↑↑ PT/PTT, ↓↓ fibrinogen, ↑↑ D-dimer, ↓ platelets. This guides blood-product replacement.

s7.q: The definitive diagnostic finding for AFE is:
options: Identification of amniotic fluid debris (fetal squamous cells, lanugo, vernix) in pulmonary vessels at autopsy
s7.explain: Autopsy finding of AF debris in pulmonary vessels is the only histopathologically confirmatory test. In survivors, diagnosis remains presumptive.
